>> other **radial · femoral · carotid · brachial · popliteal · dorsalis pedis.**

---

**Why so many?**

- **Radio-radial** delay → subclavian/supravalvular AS
- **Radio-femoral** delay → **coarctation** (the big one)

>> **Feeling the femoral is compulsory.** It is on the sheet because coarctation is missed without it.

---

## Step 5: Blood pressure — get the cuff right

Position → cuff → find brachial → inflate → **systolic by palpation first** → stethoscope → BP by auscultation.

>> **Systolic by palpation first** avoids missing the **auscultatory gap.** And remember the cuff: **width 40%, length 80–100%** of the arm.

---

## Step 6: JVP — the three marked steps

>> **Position at 45° → locate the internal jugular vein → measure the JVP.**

Remember: from the **sternal angle**, normal **< 8 cm H₂O.**

---

## Step 7: Precordium — inspect, then palpate, then percuss

- **Inspect** — precordial bulge, scars, visible pulsations
- **Palpate** the **apex beat** (position and character)
- **Palpate** for **heave and thrill** (remember: base of fingers for thrills, heel of hand for heaves)
- **Percuss** for cardiac dullness
- **Palpate the liver** and check for **ascites (shifting dullness)**

---

>> **Why liver and ascites in a HEART exam?** Because **right heart failure** causes **hepatomegaly and ascites.** The abdomen is part of the cardiovascular examination.

---

## Step 8: Auscultation — be systematic

- Start at the **apex** — listen to the heart sounds
- **Count the heart rate**
- **All 4 valve areas** — aortic, pulmonary, tricuspid, mitral
- **Radiation** — axilla (MR), up the neck (AS), back (coarctation)
- **Abdomen** and **base of the lungs** (for the crepitations of heart failure)

---

>> **Listening at the lung bases** completes the picture — pulmonary oedema from left heart failure lives there.

---

## Step 9: The non-technical marks — do not lose them

The check-off also scores: **overall attention to sequence, composure, speed of performance, courtesy, and affect.**

>> **Stay calm, be gentle, be systematic.** A flustered, rough, out-of-order examination loses marks even if the findings are right.

---

## Putting it all together

**Courtesy → Inspection → Pulse → BP → JVP → Precordium → Auscultation** — and the **sequence itself is marked.**

Introduce and position at **45°** first; **name every inspection finding**; **feel the femorals** for coarctation; get the **cuff size right**; measure the **JVP from the sternal angle**; examine the **liver and for ascites** as part of the heart exam; and auscultate **all four areas plus radiation and the lung bases.**

Then hold your **composure** — because calmness, gentleness and order are scored too.
cards.tie: This is your exact OSPE marking scheme, taken from your lecturer's own check-off sheet, so practising in this order is practising to the marks. The commonest ways students lose marks are skipping the courtesy, forgetting to feel the femoral pulses, and not examining the abdomen and lung bases — all of which are explicitly scored items.

cards.q: KEY CUES: What words and findings in a cardiovascular question should immediately point you to a specific diagnosis or answer?
cards.lecturer: Key cues: blue tongue/mucous membranes = central cyanosis (heart or lung disease); blue skin only, cold limb = peripheral cyanosis. Irregularly irregular pulse = atrial fibrillation. Slow-rising pulse (parvus et tardus) = aortic stenosis. Collapsing/water-hammer pulse = aortic regurgitation or PDA. Pulsus alternans = severe LV failure. Pulsus paradoxus (BP falls >10 on inspiration) = cardiac tamponade. Radio-femoral delay + higher BP in arms = coarctation of the aorta. Bounding pulse (wide PP) = AR, PDA, AV fistula. Thready pulse (narrow PP) = CCF, pericardial effusion. Raised JVP + muffled sounds + hypotension = Beck's triad = tamponade. Kussmaul's sign (JVP rises on inspiration) = constrictive pericarditis. Heaving apex = pressure overload/hypertrophy (AS, hypertension). Hyperdynamic apex = volume overload/dilatation (AR, MR, VSD). Tapping apex = mitral stenosis. Fixed split S2 = ASD. Wide split S2 = pulmonary stenosis. Reversed split S2 = aortic stenosis/HOCM. Pansystolic murmur = MR, TR, VSD. Ejection systolic murmur = AS, PS, HOCM. Continuous machinery murmur = PDA. Early diastolic murmur = AR. Mid-diastolic murmur = MS. Any diastolic murmur = pathological. Murmur louder on standing/Valsalva = HOCM or MVP. Lithium in pregnancy = Ebstein's anomaly. Maternal SLE = congenital heart block. Maternal diabetes = TGA/VSD. Down syndrome = ECD/VSD. Turner = coarctation. Marfan = AR/MVP. Noonan = PS.
cards.explain:
## Group 1 — Cyanosis cues

>> **Blue TONGUE/mucous membranes** → **CENTRAL** cyanosis → heart or lung disease

>> **Blue skin only, cold limb, clears with warmth** → **PERIPHERAL** cyanosis

---

## Group 2 — Pulse cues (very high yield)

>> **Irregularly irregular** → **ATRIAL FIBRILLATION**

>> **Slow-rising (parvus et tardus)** → **AORTIC STENOSIS**

>> **Collapsing / water-hammer** → **AORTIC REGURGITATION** (or PDA)

>> **Pulsus alternans** (strong-weak) → **severe LV FAILURE**

>> **Pulsus paradoxus** (BP falls > 10 on inspiration) → **CARDIAC TAMPONADE**

>> **Bounding (wide PP)** → **AR, PDA, AV fistula**

>> **Thready (narrow PP)** → **CCF, pericardial effusion**

>> **Radio-femoral delay + arm BP > leg BP** → **COARCTATION**

---

## Group 3 — JVP cues

>> **Raised JVP + muffled sounds + hypotension** → **BECK'S TRIAD → tamponade**

>> **Kussmaul's sign** (JVP rises on inspiration) → **constrictive pericarditis / tamponade**

---

## Group 4 — Apex beat cues

>> **HEAVING** (sustained) → **pressure overload → HYPERTROPHY** (AS, hypertension)

>> **HYPERDYNAMIC** (diffuse) → **volume overload → DILATATION** (AR, MR, VSD)

>> **TAPPING** → **MITRAL STENOSIS** (palpable S1)

>> **Parasternal heave** → **RVH or left atrial enlargement**

---

## Group 5 — Second heart sound cues

>> **FIXED split** → **ASD** (the classic sign)

>> **WIDE split** → **PULMONARY STENOSIS**

>> **REVERSED split** → **AORTIC STENOSIS / HOCM**

---

## Group 6 — Murmur cues (the core)

>> **Pansystolic** → **MR, TR, VSD** ("leaks and holes")

>> **Ejection-systolic** → **AS, PS, HOCM** ("tight valves")

>> **Early diastolic** → **AR, PR**

>> **Mid-diastolic** → **MITRAL STENOSIS**

>> **Continuous machinery murmur** → **PDA**

>> **ANY diastolic murmur** → **PATHOLOGICAL** (never innocent)

>> **Louder on STANDING or VALSALVA** → **HOCM or MVP**

---

## Group 7 — The "which defect" associations

**Drugs in pregnancy:**

>> **Lithium** → **Ebstein's anomaly**

>> **Alcohol** → VSD (fetal alcohol)

**Maternal conditions:**

>> **SLE** → **congenital heart block**

>> **Diabetes** → **TGA, VSD**

**Syndromes:**

>> **Down's** → **ECD, VSD**

>> **Turner** → **COARCTATION**

>> **Marfan** → **AR, MVP**

>> **Noonan** → **PS**

---

## Group 8 — The rules that decide an answer

>> **Every diastolic murmur is pathological.**

>> **Thrill = grade 4 or above = organic lesion.**

>> **Cuff too small → falsely high BP; too big → falsely low.**

>> **Innocent murmur = the S's, < grade 3, systolic, symptomless.**

>> **Central cyanosis is never normal.**

---

## Putting it all together

Three questions unlock most CVS cases:

**1. Is the murmur systolic or diastolic, and what shape?** Pansystolic → leaks/holes; ejection → stenosis; any diastolic → pathological.

**2. Is the overload pressure or volume?** Heaving/hypertrophy vs hyperdynamic/dilatation.

**3. What's the one-liner cue?** Fixed split → ASD; radio-femoral delay → coarctation; pulsus paradoxus → tamponade; louder on standing → HOCM.

Then reach for the associations — **Lithium-Ebstein, SLE-heart block, Down's-ECD, Turner-coarctation** — for the "which defect" questions.
cards.tie: These cues are the shortcut between reading a cardiology question and knowing the answer. Almost every CVS question is built around one or two of them, and recognising the cue instantly tells you which lesion is being tested and which part of the examination will confirm it.
